text: This is a sixty-six-year-old patient who presented with a new diagnosis of a colon cancer in the left colon. We did a left hepatectomy and preoperatively we had obtained a CT scan which demonstrated a liver metastasis in the right lobe of the liver. We biopsied this intraoperatively and it was consistent with cancer and postoperatively he underwent aggressive adjuvant chemotherapy with an excellent response in the liver tumor. So now we're here today to resect that area with the entire right liver.

The surgeon has opened the abdomen and mobilized the liver, removing the gallbladder. The junction of the right and left liver is visible at the gallbladder fossa, with forceps indicating the area. The right hepatic artery has been ligated, and the cystic duct stump is visible. The surgeon prepares to ligate the right branch of the portal vein, located beneath the current surgical field.
The surgeon places a stapler across the right portal vein and clamps it down, successfully stapling and cutting the vessel. The stapled and cut right portal vein is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the main portal vein and the left portal vein, noting the liver's demarcation with the right side appearing darker due to inflow cutoff. The area where the gallbladder was is visible, with normal liver color observed. The liver is mobilized off the inferior vena cava up to the right hepatic vein, preparing for resection through the liver due to anatomical considerations.

The surgeon cauterizes the area around the old ligamentum teres, preparing to enter the ischemic part of the liver using RF devices. The RF devices are used to coagulate tissue and occlude vessels smaller than three millimeters, while larger vessels may bleed during the procedure.
